properties.name: Bipartite
properties.description: A graph is **bipartite** if its vertex set can be partitioned into two independent sets; equivalently, it contains no odd cycle.

properties.name: Planar
properties.description: A graph is **planar** if it can be drawn in the plane with no two edges crossing.

properties.name: Perfect
properties.description: A graph is **perfect** if every induced subgraph $H$ satisfies $\chi(H) = \omega(H)$, i.e. its chromatic number equals its clique number.

properties.name: Chordal
properties.description: A graph is **chordal** if every cycle of length at least 4 has a chord (an edge joining two non-consecutive vertices of the cycle).

properties.name: Forest
properties.description: A graph is a **forest** if it contains no cycles. A connected forest is a tree.

properties.name: Complete
properties.description: A graph is **complete** if every pair of distinct vertices is joined by an edge.

spaces.name: Complete graph $K_5$
spaces.description: The complete graph on five vertices: every pair of the five vertices is joined by an edge.

spaces.name: Cycle $C_5$
spaces.description: The cycle on five vertices: a single closed walk $v_1 v_2 v_3 v_4 v_5 v_1$ and nothing more.

spaces.name: Claw $K_{1,3}$
spaces.description: The claw: one centre vertex joined to three leaves, with no other edges. The smallest star that is not a path.

theorems.description: A forest contains no cycles, hence no odd cycle, so it is bipartite.
A forest is acyclic and can always be drawn in the plane without crossings, so it is planar.
Bipartite graphs are perfect: every induced subgraph is bipartite, with $\chi = \omega \le 2$.
Chordal graphs are perfect (Hajnal–Surányi; Berge). Every induced subgraph of a chordal graph is chordal, and a chordal graph has a simplicial elimination ordering giving $\chi = \omega$.

traits.description: $K_5$ contains triangles (odd cycles), so it is not bipartite.
$K_5$ is non-planar; it is one of Kuratowski's two forbidden minors.
In $K_5$ every pair of vertices is adjacent, so every cycle of length at least 4 has a chord; complete graphs are chordal.
$K_5$ contains cycles, so it is not a forest.
$C_5$ is an odd cycle, hence not bipartite.
$C_5$ is a single cycle and draws in the plane without crossings.

$C_5$ is an odd hole: $\chi(C_5) = 3 > 2 = \omega(C_5)$, so it is not perfect. This is the witness that *perfect* is a strictly smaller class than *bipartite* and *chordal* — every space above derives perfection, but $C_5$ has neither defining property and fails it.
$C_5$ is an induced 5-cycle with no chord, so it is not chordal.
$C_5$ contains a cycle, so it is not a forest.
$C_5$ has non-adjacent vertices, so it is not complete.
$C_4$ is an even cycle, hence bipartite.
$C_4$ is a single cycle, drawable in the plane without crossings.
$C_4$ is an induced 4-cycle with no chord, so it is not chordal. (Together with its bipartiteness, this shows *chordal* and *bipartite* are incomparable classes.)
$C_4$ contains a cycle, so it is not a forest.
$C_4$ has non-adjacent (opposite) vertices, so it is not complete.
The claw $K_{1,3}$ is a tree, hence a forest. (This is the only trait authored for the claw — the viewer derives bipartite, planar, and perfect from the inclusion theorems.)
The leaves of the claw are pairwise non-adjacent, so it is not complete.
